title: Cheeseburger
subtitle: A smash-style beef patty with melted cheese on a brioche bun
excerpt: A proper smash-style cheeseburger: beef mince pressed hard onto a hot griddle so the edges lacquer and the centre stays juicy, a slice of American-style cheese melted directly over the top. Condiments are kept simple. Ready in ten minutes.
sourceNotes: The smash burger method was popularised in American diners in the mid-twentieth century, particularly by chains using flat-top griddles in the 1950s, 1980s. The core technique (pressing a loosely formed ball of beef mince against a screaming-hot flat iron surface) appears in working descriptions of American short-order cooking from that era. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) includes minced beef preparations that establish the foundational American approach to ground beef cookery.

content.text: The smash burger is not a style choice; it is a technique. A loose ball of beef mince goes onto a searingly hot, lightly oiled cast-iron surface and is pressed flat and hard within the first ten seconds. The contact produces a Maillard crust across nearly the entire face of the patty, lacquered, dark-edged, faintly crisp. The centre stays moist because the patty is thin and cooks fast. A slice of cheese goes on immediately after flipping, and the burger is served before the cheese has time to fully set.

Beef mince at 20 per cent fat works best. The fat bastes the patty from the inside as it cooks and prevents the crust from going dry. Season the balls of mince with salt just before pressing, salt draws moisture out, and if you season too early you'll get a rubbery texture. The bun should be briefly toasted in the beef fat left in the pan.

items.name: Cheddar
items.prepNote: American cheese or mature cheddar, sliced

content.text: Heat the pan and prepare the buns

Set a cast-iron skillet over the highest heat and leave it for 3 to 4 minutes until smoking. It should feel intensely hot when you hold your hand 5 cm above the surface. Meanwhile, season each ball of mince generously on all sides with salt and black pepper just before cooking.

Place the {{beef-mince}} balls into the dry pan (no oil needed at 20% fat) and immediately press each one flat with a fish slice, holding firm pressure for 10 seconds. The patty should be no more than 1 cm thick. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes without moving until the edges look lacquered and dark. Flip once. Lay a slice of cheese over each patty and cook for a further 90 seconds.

Remove the patties and rest for 1 minute. Add the bun halves, cut-side down, to the still-hot pan and toast for 30 to 45 seconds until golden. Spread {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise on the base and {{ketchup}} of ketchup on the crown. Add lettuce, tomato, and pickles if using. Place the patty on the base and press the lid gently on.

items.fix: Heat the cast-iron skillet for at least 3 to 4 minutes over maximum heat before cooking; the surface should be visibly smoking
items.cause: Pan was not hot enough when the mince went in
items.symptom: Crust is pale and the patty steamed rather than fried

items.fix: Use 20% fat mince; flip only once and serve immediately after the second side is done
items.cause: Mince too lean (under 15% fat), or overcooked
items.symptom: Patty is dry in the centre

items.fix: Add the cheese immediately after flipping and keep the heat on; a thin slice of processed cheese melts faster than a thick cut of aged cheddar
items.cause: Cheese added after removing from heat, or too thick a slice

items.fix: Keep the balls loose, not packed; press within the first 10 seconds of contact with the hot pan
items.cause: Mince was overworked or patty not pressed flat immediately

content.text: The smash burger method came out of American diners in the mid-twentieth century. The tools were a flat-top griddle and a metal spatula, and the kitchens worked fast. The thin patty meets more of the hot griddle and builds a crust that a thick, loose-cooked burger never quite gets. Mayonnaise, ketchup, and a soft bun is the American diner template. Simple, it works, and there's not much to argue with. It suits beef of any grade, but good mince makes a real difference. Aim for coarse-ground chuck at 20 per cent fat for the best result.
